questions.question: What does TAO stand for and why is it a strong signal in a Meta interview?
options: The Associations and Objects graph cache — naming it shows you understand Meta serves the social graph from a two-level cache, not directly from MySQL.
TAO is Meta's time-series analytics platform, similar to Prometheus.
TAO is Meta's container orchestration system, equivalent to Kubernetes.
TAO stands for Traffic Aware Orchestration and routes API requests to the nearest datacenter.
questions.explanation: TAO (The Associations and Objects) is Meta's two-level distributed graph cache. Knowing it signals that you understand the social graph is not served from MySQL and that objects and associations are separate primitives with different access patterns.

questions.question: Why does Meta's news feed use a hybrid push/pull fanout strategy rather than pure push-on-write?
options: Pure push-on-write would require writing a post to millions of feed caches for celebrity accounts, overwhelming the fanout pipeline.
Pull-on-read is always faster because it avoids the write amplification entirely.
Push-on-write is only used for paid promotional posts; organic posts always use pull-on-read.
The hybrid approach is only needed for video posts, not text or photo posts.
questions.explanation: A user with 50M followers triggers 50M cache writes on a single post. Above a follower threshold, Meta switches to pull-on-read so the feed is assembled on demand, avoiding the fanout storm.

questions.question: Which pattern is the clearest weak-hire signal in a Meta interview?
options: Applying push-on-write uniformly to all users including celebrities without mentioning the follower-count threshold problem.
Starting with a capacity estimate and naming graph edge count before drawing the architecture.
Mentioning TAO as the graph cache layer and distinguishing objects from associations.
Identifying privacy propagation as a synchronous constraint before fan-out begins.
questions.explanation: Ignoring the celebrity/high-follower write amplification problem shows the candidate has not thought about the real bottleneck in news feed fanout. It is the first scaling problem Meta engineers encounter.

questions.question: A strong-hire answer at the 15-minute mark in a Meta interview looks like which of the following?
options: An end-to-end sketch with named services (graph serving, fanout, ranking, privacy), plus a stated fanout strategy for normal vs. high-follower accounts.
A fully detailed data model for every service including MySQL schema and index definitions.
A list of Meta technologies to use without explaining the trade-off each one resolves.
A deep dive into the TAO cache eviction algorithm with no mention of other subsystems.
questions.explanation: By 15 minutes, the interviewer needs to see breadth: named services with clear ownership boundaries and the critical fanout strategy decision. Depth comes after the structure is agreed upon.

questions.question: Why must privacy enforcement happen before fan-out begins, not at read time?
options: If fan-out starts before the privacy change is confirmed, a post could propagate to feed caches of users outside the intended audience, creating an audit gap that is hard to close.
Read-time enforcement is computationally too expensive for Meta's scale.
Privacy enforcement at read time requires the TAO cache, which is only available for write operations.
Fan-out and privacy enforcement are handled by the same service, so order does not matter.
questions.explanation: Feed caches are populated asynchronously. If a post fans out before the audience restriction is applied, it can be visible to the wrong audience before the correction propagates. Blocking fan-out on privacy confirmation eliminates this window.

questions.question: How does the TAO two-level cache architecture work for a social graph read?
options: Clients read from TAO followers; on a miss, followers fetch from TAO leaders; on a leader miss, leaders fetch from MySQL shards. Writes go to leaders and propagate down.
TAO is a write-through cache: every read goes to MySQL, which populates TAO asynchronously.
TAO followers and leaders are identical replicas; the two levels exist only for geographic redundancy.
TAO leaders handle association queries; TAO followers handle object queries; MySQL is not involved in reads.
questions.explanation: TAO has two cache levels: followers (closer to app servers) and leaders (one per region). Reads start at followers, fall through to leaders on a miss, and finally hit MySQL shards if the leader also misses. Writes always go to the leader.

questions.question: What is the correct role of ML ranking in a Meta-style news feed design?
options: The ranking model consumes pre-computed features from a feature store and scores candidate posts; it must be designed as an independently deployable service so models can be updated and A/B tested without redeploying feed retrieval.
Ranking is applied at the CDN layer to avoid adding latency to the origin servers.
ML ranking only applies to ads, not organic feed content.
Ranking is embedded inline in the database query so the result set is pre-sorted before reaching the application.
questions.explanation: Feed ranking is a first-class service at Meta, separate from feed retrieval. Decoupling them allows independent model iteration, A/B testing, and failure isolation: if ranking is down, the feed can fall back to recency-sorted results.

questions.question: Which failure mode is most likely to require immediate privacy-aware remediation rather than eventual consistency?
options: A post's audience setting changes from public to friends-only: the new restriction must be enforced on the next read, not after a cache TTL expires.
A TAO follower diverges from its leader: the stale friend list can be served for a short window without harm.
A feed ranking model serves slightly stale engagement features: CTR impact is measurable but not a privacy violation.
A photo upload is delayed in the Haystack pipeline: eventual delivery is acceptable for non-real-time media.
questions.explanation: Audience restriction changes are a hard privacy requirement at Meta. Serving a post to the wrong audience — even briefly — is a data exposure incident. All other cases tolerate eventual consistency; privacy does not.
